1: 'Yet one plague more (nega echad od) I will bring upon Pharaoh and upon Egypt': The designation of the final event as a nega (H5061, blow/plague/stroke) rather than a makkah (plague/blow) or ot (sign) marks it categorically differently from the first nine. Nega is used throughout Leviticus 13-14 for the 'stroke' of skin disease that necessitates priestly examination — it carries a clinical precision. The announcement 'after that he will let you go; when he lets you go he will drive you out completely' (garesh yegaresh) contains an intensified repetition (infinitive absolute + verb) signaling absolute certainty and violent compulsion.
2: 'Speak now in the hearing of the people, that they ask (yish'alu), every man of his neighbor and every woman of her neighbor, for silver and gold jewelry': The Hebrew sha'al (H7592) means to ask/request, not to borrow in the modern sense; the LXX uses aiteō (to ask), not a loan-word. The spoiling of Egypt fulfills YHWH's promise to Abraham in Genesis 15:14: 'afterward they shall come out with great possessions (rekush gadol).' The silver and gold transferred from Egyptian neighbors represents a form of back-wages for generations of slave labor — a restitution embedded within the structure of the exodus itself.
3: YHWH gave the people favor (chen) in the sight of the Egyptians: Chen (H2580, grace/favor/charm) — the same word used for Noah finding favor with God (Genesis 6:8), for Joseph finding favor with Potiphar (Genesis 39:4), and for Esther finding favor before Ahasuerus (Esther 2:17). In each case the socially inferior person is given unaccountable acceptance from a superior. The Egyptian neighbors' willingness to give their valuables to the departing Israelites is supernaturally explained: YHWH disposed them to generosity. 'Moreover, the man Moses was very great in the land of Egypt in the sight of Pharaoh's servants and in the sight of the people' — Moses's stature has shifted from supplicant to most-respected figure in the land.
4: Moses said, 'Thus says YHWH: About midnight (kachatzot halailah) I will go out through the midst of Egypt': Kachatzot (approximately at the dividing/middle of the night) — the Hebrew notes this is an approximation, not an exact hour-specification. This was important apologetically in later Jewish interpretation: if the announcement said 'exactly at midnight' and Pharaoh's court noted the death occurred slightly before or after, they might claim a discrepancy. The timing at midnight echoes the midnight-ness of the Passover Seder as instituted later — Jews eat the Passover lamb before midnight. The fact that YHWH himself goes out (ani yotze) — not an angel, not a plague — makes the tenth plague categorically unique: it is a direct divine action, a theophanic event of judgment.
5: 'Every firstborn in the land of Egypt shall die, from the firstborn of Pharaoh who sits on his throne, even to the firstborn of the slave girl who is behind the hand mill, and all the firstborn of the livestock': The social range — from Pharaoh to the lowest female slave (shiphchah asher achar harehayim, literally 'the slave woman who is behind the millstones') — inverts the plague's expected theological target. Pharaoh, as the divine son of Ra, was the most symbolically loaded firstborn in the kingdom; his heir was equally divine. The fact that Egypt's most sacred royal institution shares the judgment with its lowest prisoner-slaves democratizes the catastrophe and destroys the theological claim that Egyptian divine kingship provided special protection.
6: 'There shall be a great cry (tze'aqah gedolah) throughout all the land of Egypt, such as there has never been, nor ever will be again': Tze'aqah (H6818, outcry/wailing) — the same root as the cry of the Israelites in their slavery (Exodus 2:23: vataal zaaqatam) and the cry of the Sodomites' victims (Genesis 18:20: za'aqat Sedom). The wilderness generation's exodus began from a cry; the liberation was achieved through a matching cry imposed on Egypt. The reversal is lexically explicit: Egypt's cry mirrors and answers Israel's cry.
7: 'But not a dog shall growl against any of the people of Israel, either man or beast, that you may know that YHWH makes a distinction between Egypt and Israel': The Hebrew idiom 'lo yecharetz-kelev leshono' (not a dog shall sharpen its tongue) means not a dog shall bark or growl — a proverbial expression for total absence of disturbance. Dogs in the ancient Near East were not pets but scavengers and security animals, and their alarm-bark was significant in military and domestic contexts. The contrast: Egypt screams, Israel is in silence. This silence is the most dramatic articulation of the pala (wonderfully distinguish/separate) principle introduced in Exodus 8:23.
8: 'And all these your servants shall come down to me and bow down to me, saying: Get out, you and all the people who follow you. And after that I will go out': Moses's prophetic statement reverses the entire power dynamic that has obtained throughout the negotiations: the Egyptian officials who have spoken to Moses with contempt ('this man,' Exodus 10:7) will prostrate themselves before him and urgently beg him to leave. The verb vehishtachavu li (bow down to me) uses the full prostration vocabulary of royal submission. The narrative closes: 'He went out from Pharaoh in hot anger (bechori af)' — Moses's anger matches the moral urgency of the moment.
9: Then YHWH said to Moses, 'Pharaoh will not listen to you, that my wonders may be multiplied in the land of Egypt': The theological explanation given retrospectively by YHWH — Pharaoh's non-response is the precondition for the multiplication of signs — reframes the entire plague narrative as divinely orchestrated. The word moftim (H4159, wonders/portents) here differs from otot (signs/tokens) — moftim emphasizes the spectacular, extraordinary, demonstrably supernatural character of the acts. In Egyptian texts, the term equivalent to moftim was applied to acts of the gods that overturned normal causality.
10: Moses and Aaron did all these wonders before Pharaoh, and YHWH hardened Pharaoh's heart, and he did not let the people of Israel go out of his land: The summary statement of the entire plague cycle — all the wonders done, Pharaoh's heart hardened by YHWH, Israel still in Egypt — is structurally a dead end. Every human and divine negotiating mechanism has been exhausted. The stage is fully set for the final unilateral divine action that no refusal can resist: the death of the firstborn. The phrase 'lo shillach et benei Yisrael me'artzo' (he did not let the sons of Israel go from his land) calls attention to the possessive: Pharaoh claims Israel as his possession; the exodus will demonstrate that they belong to YHWH.
